terms.def: A reference index used to evaluate portfolio performance and calibrate risk; must be investable and representative.
Std dev of active returns vs. the benchmark; measures how closely the portfolio follows the benchmark.
½ × Σ|w_portfolio − w_benchmark|; % of portfolio holdings that differ from the benchmark.
Closet indexing: low active share, low tracking error — taking active fees for near-index exposure.
Major index families; MSCI for global equity, Russell for US small/large cap, Bloomberg for fixed income.
Global Industry Classification Standard; used by most equity benchmarks to define sector composition.

eli3: A benchmark is the standard against which a portfolio's performance is measured. Choosing the right benchmark is critical: if your benchmark is wrong, your performance attribution is meaningless, your risk limits are miscalibrated, and you incentivise the wrong manager behaviour. A global equity fund measured against the S&P 500 will always look like it's taking huge country risk — but it's the benchmark that's wrong.

A good benchmark satisfies five criteria (SAMURAI): Specified in advance, Appropriate (reflects the investment universe), Measurable (returns observable), Unambiguous (clear composition), Investable (can be replicated passively), and Accountable (manager accepts it as fair). Custom benchmarks, factor benchmarks, and liability benchmarks are used when standard indices don't match the mandate.

In asset management, selecting the benchmark also determines the manager's incentives. Tracking error and active return are measured against the benchmark — if you choose a too-easy benchmark, the manager looks skilled; too-hard, and even good managers appear to fail. Benchmark choice is a governance decision, not just a technical one.

whenWhy.title: Standard benchmark types
whenWhy.body: Major benchmark families and their applications: Equity: (1) US equity: S&P 500 (large cap), Russell 2000 (small cap), Russell 3000 (total market), Wilshire 5000 (broadest US); (2) International: MSCI EAFE (developed ex-US), MSCI EM (emerging markets), MSCI ACWI (all-country world); (3) Style: Russell 1000 Value/Growth split by P/B and earnings growth. Fixed income: (1) US: Bloomberg US Aggregate (investment-grade bonds: Treasuries + agencies + MBS + IG corporate); (2) HY: Bloomberg US Corporate HY; (3) Global: Bloomberg Global Aggregate; (4) EM: JPMorgan EMBI (sovereign dollar), GBI-EM (local currency government). Real assets: FTSE NAREIT (REITs), Bloomberg Commodity Index, NCREIF ODCE (private real estate). Hedge funds: HFRI indices (hedge fund composites by strategy — not truly investable). Key: choose the benchmark that best matches the manager's investable universe and style. Misalignment between mandate and benchmark creates benchmark risk — the portfolio deviating from benchmark despite performing well on its own terms.

whenWhy.title: Custom and factor benchmarks
whenWhy.body: When standard indices don't fit: (1) Blended benchmarks: 60% MSCI World + 40% Bloomberg Global Agg for a balanced fund. Custom composite updated quarterly as target allocation changes. (2) Custom index: build an index from a defined universe with specific rules. Example: 'large-cap US equities with ESG screens' — exclude tobacco, weapons, score by ESG rating. Often constructed by MSCI, FTSE Russell, or S&P on request. (3) Factor benchmarks: for smart beta or factor-tilted funds, the benchmark is the factor itself. A low-volatility fund's benchmark is MSCI Minimum Volatility Index. A value fund's benchmark might be MSCI World Value. (4) Liability benchmark: for LDI (liability-driven investing), the benchmark is the present value of the fund's liabilities — typically a duration-matched Treasury or corporate bond index. Tracking error is measured against liability changes, not an asset index. (5) Absolute return: cash + 5% p.a. as benchmark — appropriate for unconstrained hedge fund-like mandates. No systematic factor exposure is 'active' — all vol is attributed to manager skill or error.

whenWhy.title: Benchmark manipulation and gaming
whenWhy.body: Benchmark selection creates incentives: managers will naturally gravitate toward positions that don't deviate from the benchmark (career risk management). This leads to: (1) Closet indexing: manager claims active management but tracks benchmark closely to avoid underperformance; (2) Benchmark manipulation: funds lobbying for changes to their benchmark to make their past performance look better; (3) Window dressing: adjusting the portfolio near reporting dates to look more like the benchmark; (4) Index construction effects: stocks added to major indices (S&P 500) experience price jumps due to passive fund buying — the inclusion effect is well documented (Harris & Gurel 1986). Benchmark changes: when an index reconstitutes (stocks added/removed), active managers must decide whether to trade — missing the reconstitution may increase TE; trading adds transaction costs. Governance: benchmarks should be set by investment committees or asset owners, not by fund managers. External benchmark committees (MSCI, Russell) ensure objectivity.

deepDiveIntro: Benchmark quality criteria — SAMURAI test: (S) Specified: defined before the measurement period, not chosen retroactively. (A) Appropriate: reflects the fund's investment universe and constraints (long-only equity fund benchmarked against a shorting index is inappropriate). (M) Measurable: returns calculable on any date. (U) Unambiguous: every observer computes the same return. (R) Reflective of investment opinions: manager can express positive/negative views relative to the benchmark. (A) Accountable: manager accepts the benchmark as a fair measure. (I) Investable: can be replicated through passive instruments at low cost. Failure on any criterion undermines performance attribution and manager evaluation.

deepDive.heading: Index Construction Methodologies
deepDive.body: Different weighting schemes create different benchmarks with different risk/return properties: (1) Market-cap weighted (S&P 500, MSCI World): weight proportional to market cap. Advantage: zero rebalancing unless the universe changes; reflects the 'market portfolio'. Disadvantage: momentum bias — overweights most expensive stocks; concentration risk (top 10 stocks = 30%+ of S&P 500 in 2024); (2) Equal-weighted (equal dollar allocation): forces regular rebalancing; higher small-cap exposure. Higher long-run returns but higher transaction costs; (3) Fundamental-weighted (RAFI/Arnott): weight by sales, dividends, book value. Tilts toward value; avoids buying the most expensive stocks. Often called 'smart beta' index; (4) Factor-weighted: Minimum volatility (min portfolio vol), Maximum Sharpe (mean-variance optimal), Risk parity (equal risk contribution). These are portfolio-construction-method benchmarks. For fund managers: the benchmark's weighting scheme determines what 'passive' means. A fundamentals-weighted benchmark makes active management harder (the passive benchmark itself has factor tilts). [1]

deepDive.heading: ESG and Custom Benchmark Construction
deepDive.body: ESG benchmarks: exclude certain sectors or companies based on environmental, social, governance criteria. Construction approaches: (1) Exclusion-based: remove tobacco, weapons, fossil fuels from standard index. Remaining stocks are re-weighted proportionally. Simple but creates systematic sector underweights; (2) Best-in-class ESG scoring: include top ESG-rated companies in each sector. Maintains sector neutrality but requires reliable ESG scores; (3) ESG integration: tilt weights toward high ESG score companies; reduce weights for low scorers. Continuous adjustment; (4) Thematic: 100% allocated to a specific theme (clean energy, water, social infrastructure). Narrow universe, high tracking error vs. broad market. Challenges: (1) ESG score divergence: MSCI, Sustainalytics, and Bloomberg ESG scores for the same company often differ substantially (Berg et al. 2022: correlation between providers ≈ 0.5); (2) Greenwashing: funds claiming ESG mandates but making minimal exclusions; (3) Carbon accounting: scope 1, 2, 3 emissions differ dramatically in coverage and reliability. Net-zero benchmarks: target a declining carbon intensity relative to base year; forces managers to track the decarbonisation path. Mandated by several major pension funds. [2]

deepDive.heading: Benchmark Risk and Portfolio Construction
deepDive.body: Benchmark risk = risk of underperforming the benchmark. In portfolio optimisation, the benchmark introduces an additional risk dimension: instead of minimising total portfolio variance, managers minimise: minimize TE² + λ × transaction costs, subject to: E[active return] ≥ target, TE ≤ limit, w_i ≥ 0 (long-only), Σ w_i = 1. This is a mean-TE² optimisation. Solution: the efficient frontier in active-return/TE² space is analogous to the Markowitz mean-variance frontier. The benchmark portfolio sits at (0, 0) — zero active return, zero TE. Moving along the frontier increases both expected active return and TE. Information ratio = slope of the frontier = max(active return per unit TE). Black-Litterman model (see entry) uses the benchmark as the prior: starting from the benchmark portfolio, apply active tilts proportional to the manager's views and conviction. Portfolios that don't use a benchmark optimisation will tend to drift from the benchmark more than necessary, incurring extra TE without commensurate active return. [1]

takeaways: SAMURAI benchmark criteria: Specified, Appropriate, Measurable, Unambiguous, Reflective, Accountable, Investable. [1]
Cap-weighted (S&P 500) vs. equal-weighted vs. factor-weighted: each creates different risk tilts and passive exposures. [1]
ESG benchmark divergence: provider correlation ≈ 0.5; best-in-class maintains sector neutrality vs. exclusion approach. [2]
Benchmark selection sets manager incentives: wrong benchmark leads to closet indexing or benchmark gaming. [1]
Liability benchmark (LDI): track present value of liabilities, not an asset index — duration-matched bond portfolio. [1]

edgeCases.q: When should a fund use an absolute return benchmark vs. a relative benchmark?
edgeCases.a: Absolute return benchmark (e.g., cash + 5%): appropriate when: (1) The fund has no systematic long-only constraint — can go long and short, use leverage, trade multiple asset classes; (2) Return objective is specified in absolute terms (e.g., endowment needs 7% to meet spending); (3) The manager has high discretion and the investment universe is too broad to define a standard benchmark; (4) Hedge fund mandates where the goal is alpha, not beta exposure. Relative benchmark (e.g., MSCI World): appropriate when: (1) The fund has systematic long-only equity exposure — the benchmark captures the beta; (2) Investors are comparing the fund to similar funds (peer group comparison); (3) Mandate requires tracking a specific index with limited active deviation. Risk: absolute return benchmark hides beta exposure. A fund earning cash + 5% might be leveraged equity with 70% equity beta — outperforms in bull markets, crashes in bear markets, but always looks like it 'beat the benchmark.' A relative benchmark would reveal the equity risk. Hybrid approach: report both TE against a market benchmark (to capture systematic risk) and IR against a return target (to capture absolute performance). Common for multi-asset portfolios.

edgeCases.q: How does benchmark rebalancing and reconstitution affect active managers?
edgeCases.a: Index reconstitution (annual or quarterly): when stocks are added to or removed from a major index (Russell annual reconstitution in June, S&P 500 additions): (1) Addition effect: index funds must buy newly added stocks on the effective date. Prices typically rise 2-5% before reconstitution as active managers front-run; (2) Deletion effect: removed stocks fall 1-3% on announcement as index funds sell; (3) Active manager dilemma: buy the addition before inclusion (capture the price jump but pay a premium) or wait (no premium, avoid overpaying, but risk TE divergence). Research shows: stocks added to S&P 500 gain +3.4% on announcement (Harris & Gurel 1986); some reversal post-inclusion; (4) Reconstitution costs: pure index funds incur significant transaction costs during reconstitutions. Well-managed index funds (e.g., Vanguard) smooth rebalancing over days to weeks to reduce market impact. Strategic active managers can exploit reconstitution predictability: Russell small-to-large migrations are highly predictable based on market-cap thresholds — positions can be taken weeks in advance. This is a documented alpha source for active managers with low transaction costs.

sources.label: Bailey, J.V. (1992) — Evaluating Benchmark Quality, FAJ
Berg, F., Koelbel, J.F. & Rigobon, R. (2022) — Aggregate Confusion: The Divergence of ESG Ratings, RoF
